multi_full_api.reasoning_trace: m001 hashrate_degradation warn/0.75, XGBoost p_drop=0.63 (below crit threshold 0.75). Hashrate agent: noise 0.82 — 1m and 6m hashrate identical at 105.33 TH/s, σ=0.23 TH/s (~0.22%), no trend visible. Temp 72.4°C, voltage 12.07V, power 3247W all stationary with minimal variance. HSI=98.81, TE=100 — healthy KPIs. Agent cites short_window_variance_false_positive pattern; predictor fired on 1-min feature window that doesn't persist at longer horizon. Matches maestro memory pattern xgboost_hashrate_fp_unanimous_noise (noise ≥0.80, tight telemetry). Primary specialist returned definitive noise — no fallback needed. m008 hashrate_degradation warn/0.7554 (XGBoost p=0.64, above warn, below crit). Hashrate agent: inconclusive conf=0.45 — hr_mean_1m = hr_mean_6m = 89.17 TH/s (stationary, low σ), HSI=96.4, pattern resembles short-window FP, but no 30-min trajectory to confirm. Hints human_review. Voltage agent: inconclusive conf=0.38 — v_mean=11.9998V, σ=0.0375V (clean, nominal), voltage is a bystander not a driver; flag is hashrate-feature-driven (HSI, TE=88.09); no 30-min telemetry available to rule out slow capacitor-wear ramp. Both specialists inconclusive due to missing sustained-window telemetry — this is a data gap problem, not a clear signal either way. Per disagreement rules: two inconclusive agents, agent stddev on confidence is high (0.45 vs 0.38), cannot resolve autonomously. Telemetry gap means I cannot apply the `xgboost_hashrate_fp_unanimous_noise` pattern (that requires ≥0.80 noise calls, not inconclusive). Reversibility principle: no autonomous action warranted when evidence is this ambiguous. Escalate L2 with recommendation to pull 30-min telemetry history for m008 and re-evaluate. No autonomous throttle without cleaner signal.

m002 hashrate_degradation crit/0.97. XGBoost fires at 96.3% p(drop≥20%/30min) — high raw score, but both specialists return inconclusive. Hashrate agent (conf=0.42): 1m and 6m hr_mean are identical at 87.7 TH/s with σ=0.33 — no actual movement yet, consistent with either a pre-drop plateau or the known short-window variance FP pattern; thermal/power/fan all stable; no 30-min telemetry to confirm trajectory. Voltage agent (conf=0.35): v_mean=12.08V, v_std=23mV — stationary rail, no drift signal; also blocked by absent 30-min history, cannot Z-score against baseline. Neither specialist can resolve signal vs. noise without realized time-series data. Memory pattern xgboost_hashrate_fp_unanimous_noise doesn't apply here — both agents are inconclusive, not unanimously noise, so FP override is not warranted. Agent stddev is ~0.05 on confidence but both are below 0.50 and explicitly inconclusive, which means I cannot autonomously throttle (L3 requires actionable signal). Escalating to L2: alert operator with recommendation to pull 30-min telemetry immediately and reassess. If follow-on data confirms slope, throttle 80%/60min is the natural next step.

m038 chip_instability_precursor warn/0.727 (xgboost_rolling_variance). Primary: hashrate_agent → inconclusive conf=0.42 ('variance stack elevated across all windows: 30m std=2.94, 5m=2.86, 1m=2.82 TH/s; temp_chip_30m_std=1.41°C mildly elevated; no telemetry time-series to confirm shape or thermal correlation — cannot classify'). Fallback: voltage_agent → inconclusive conf=0.35 ('no voltage rail data in telemetry payload; cannot rule out PSU sag or cap wear as driver; pattern consistent with chip-level instability but unconfirmable'). Both specialists independently hit inconclusive due to missing telemetry (empty miner_recent_telemetry_30min and miner_recent_kpis_30min). Agent stddev ≈0.05 — tight agreement on uncertainty, not on signal. Per disagreement rules: >1 inconclusive agent → escalate. Cannot execute L3 throttle without at least one real_signal verdict. Action: alert operator, recommend pulling live voltage + hashrate telemetry for m038 and re-running within next cycle. If variance persists at 30m window, escalate to throttle L3.

m012 chip_instability_precursor crit/0.9997. XGBoost fired near-certainty on multi-scale variance: hashrate 30m_std=3.03 TH/s, power 30m_std=95.9W, chip temp 30m_std=1.44°C — pattern consistent with sawtooth/oscillation or intermittent stall cluster, not stationary noise. Hashrate_agent (primary): inconclusive/0.38 — confirms variance is real and multi-scale but telemetry window empty, cannot classify trajectory. Voltage_agent (fallback): inconclusive/0.38 — could be 12V rail instability but no voltage traces to confirm; equally consistent with thermal throttle/DVFS/dying ASIC. Both specialists agree the signal is real; neither can attribute root cause due to missing telemetry backfill. Override of first-pass L4: escalating a crit flag to human review without taking any protective action on the miner is the wrong trade. The variance spike is unambiguous even if root cause isn't — throttle to 80%/60min is reversible, reduces thermal and electrical stress across all plausible failure modes (thermal, PSU, hashboard), and buys time for telemetry backfill + operator inspection. Pair with L2 alert so operator investigates the telemetry gap and inspects voltage/thermal traces within the hour. L3 bounded action is exactly the reversibility-first play here; L4-only would leave the miner running at full load under a 0.9997 precursor signal while waiting for human hands.

m009 fan_anomaly warn/0.88. Fan idx=2 reading 484 RPM vs 3000 RPM threshold — 84% below threshold over a 2-min window. Raw score 1.0. Environment agent assessed noise (conf=0.72, info): argues miner-local mechanical/firmware issue, no ambient drift or zone cluster data to corroborate, recommends observe. Per routing table, fan_anomaly has no fallback — fan failures are unambiguous. I disagree with the specialist's noise call here: the flag source is a rule engine at confidence 0.88 with raw_score 1.0, and a fan spinning at 484 RPM is a hardware failure signal, not telemetry jitter. The environment agent correctly scoped this as miner-local, but mis-classified the severity. A fan running at ~16% of threshold while the chip workload continues is a credible thermal runaway precursor. However, I do not have current chip temp data to confirm imminent thermal danger, which prevents me from autonomously throttling with high confidence. Alerting operator to investigate m009 fan idx=2, recommend physical inspection and consider throttle to reduce thermal load pending resolution. L2 — ambiguous enough on thermal state to keep a human in the loop, but urgent enough not to log-and-ignore.

m901 fan_anomaly crit/0.94. Environment agent (primary, no fallback for fan faults): real_signal 0.92 crit — fan idx 1 stalled (0 RPM), fan idx 3 at 612 RPM vs 3000 threshold; chip temp 79.2°C climbing 1.1°C/min. Miner-local hardware fault with active thermal trajectory (~+10°C in 10 min at current slope). Agent hinted alert_operator; I override toward the less intrusive *and* more protective action: throttle 70%/30min is reversible, within L3 floor, immediately reduces heat generation, and buys time for physical fan replacement. Operator alert fires in parallel — physical intervention is still required; throttle does not substitute for it, only protects the miner in the interim. Not L4: throttle is reversible and magnitude-capped; shutdown is not warranted while chip is still 6°C below typical 85°C trip. If chip temp crosses 85°C or fan_3 also stalls on next cycle, escalate to L4 shutdown. Confirming first-pass Sonnet decision.

m031 thermal_runaway crit/0.95. Rule engine: chip 87.97°C, +2.97°C over threshold, sustained ramp +0.8°C/min across 10min window — real thermal, not transient. Environment (Haiku) inconclusive conf=0.35: no ambient/zone peer data to rule site-wide; hints human_review. Voltage (Sonnet) inconclusive conf=0.32: no rail/power telemetry to assess PSU contribution; hints human_review. Critically, neither specialist called noise — their inconclusives are from missing data, not disconfirming evidence. The thermal itself is unambiguous and escalating; waiting on human approval while chip climbs ~0.8°C/min risks crossing hardware damage thresholds. Flow 2 protective path: throttle 70%/30min is reversible, magnitude-capped, directly reduces thermal load, and buys time. Concurrently alert operator — site-wide HVAC event cannot be ruled out and zone peers must be checked; if >=3 co-located miners trend similarly, operator escalates to fleet-wide L4. L4 shutdown disproportionate: threshold breach alone, no evidence of imminent failure. Confirming first-pass L3 throttle + operator alert.

m902 thermal_runaway crit/0.97 (rule_engine). Environment specialist: real_signal 0.92, severity crit — chip 91.4°C (+6.4°C over 85°C threshold), plateaued 12min flat (trend 0.0°C/min), 18% hashrate drop, miner-local signature (plateau + hashrate correlation, not ambient-driven). Specialist hint: alert_operator.

Synthesis: fault has completed escalation. Plateau at 91.4°C with zero trend means thermal equilibrium has been reached at an unsafe operating point — physical failure (fan, heatsink contact, thermal paste, PSU coupling) is the most likely root cause. L3 throttle to 70% is a false-comfort action here: with headroom exhausted and no recovery trend, bounded throttle will not restore safe operating temps and delays the physical intervention the miner actually needs. L2 alert also understates severity — this isn't ambiguous.

Confirming first-pass: L4 human_review. Operator decides scheduled vs emergency shutdown and coordinates crew for physical inspection. Not auto-queuing shutdown — L4_always rule applies, and operator may want to sequence power-down with on-site presence.

m903 voltage_drift warn/0.76. Rail at 11.63V, -3.08% below nominal 12.0V, 70mV below 11.7V warn threshold. Hashrate slightly degraded (101.2 vs 104.0 TH/s, -2.7%) but not collapsed.

Voltage agent: inconclusive conf=0.42 — absolute value genuinely concerning, but 30-min telemetry and 7-day history both empty; cannot classify drift shape (step/ramp/oscillation), cannot rule in/out thermal ramp, cap wear, or upstream grid event. No episodic memory matches. Recommends human_review.

Power agent: inconclusive conf=0.38 — zone peer telemetry empty, cannot compute rack correlation or distinguish miner-local PSU fault from zonal/upstream PDU sag. -3.08% drift above warn threshold (2%) but below crit (5%). Recommends human_review.

Synthesis: Per operating rules, primary (voltage_agent) returned inconclusive → fallback (power_agent) consulted. Both inconclusive with low confidence (0.42, 0.38; stddev ~0.03, tight agreement in uncertainty). No trajectory data, no peer telemetry, no episodic memory — data gaps block causal attribution entirely. The absolute voltage reading is real and below threshold; this is not noise. But without shape or scope data, autonomous throttle (L3) would act on incomplete information. Both specialists explicitly recommend human_review. Escalating to L2: alert operator with full trace. Operator should check PDU peer miners manually and inspect PSU health on m903. If trajectory or peer data becomes available and trend is ramping, throttle 80%/60min would be appropriate next step.

m903 voltage_drift warn/0.76, rule_engine. Magnitude is real: 11.63V is 70mV below the 11.7V warn threshold (-3.08%), sits between 2% warn and 5% crit bands, hashrate off by ~2.7%. L1 observe is too passive given the threshold breach + hashrate drop. L2 alert operator with explicit data requests is the right call: surface the gap, let human pull telemetry, re-flag if confirmed isolated. No prior episodic memory for m903 to lean on.

single_sonnet.reasoning_trace:
**Voltage rail:** v_mean_1m=12.0657V, v_std_1m=0.0177V — nominal 12V rail, essentially rock-solid. No PSU-side anomaly. Voltage is not the driver.

**Hashrate trajectory:** hr_mean_1m=105.33 TH/s == hr_mean_6m=105.33 TH/s, hr_std_1m=0.2313 == hr_std_6m=0.2313. The 1-min and 6-min rolling means and std-devs are *identical* — this is a snapshot where the miner has been stable for at least 6 minutes. There is no observable degradation trend in the current window; no sustained variance increase, no downward trajectory. The predicted 20% drop probability is 0.6297, sitting between warn (0.50) and critical (0.75) thresholds.

**Thermal/environment:** temp_mean_1m=72.36°C, temp_std=0.18°C — warm but stable. hsi=98.81 (hashrate stability index near-perfect), te=100.0 (thermal efficiency at ceiling — fans working hard). fan_mean=5889.5 RPM — fans are elevated, consistent with thermal load management at 72°C. No HVAC excursion visible.

**Power:** power_mean_1m=3247.79W, power_std=14.14W — nominal power draw, low variance. No rack-level anomaly.

**Synthesis:** All rolling features are internally consistent and stable. The 1m == 6m identity suggests this is an early/flat window where the model is pattern-matching on absolute feature values (elevated temp, high fan speed, high power) rather than detecting an actual degrading trajectory. The raw_score=0.6297 is sub-critical (threshold 0.75), severity=warn, model confidence=0.7519. No single domain (voltage, thermal, power, hashrate) shows an actionable signal today. 

**Autonomy ruling:** Evidence is below critical threshold, no clear causal driver, all signals stable. Reversibility is moot — there is nothing to act on yet. Taking autonomous throttle (L3) would be premature and unsupported by trajectory data. Alerting operator (L2) would add noise for a warn-level, sub-critical, stable snapshot. Correct disposition is L1_observe: log, continue monitoring, re-evaluate if hr_mean_1m begins diverging from hr_mean_6m or temp/fan trends worsen.

**Voltage domain:** v_mean_1m=11.9998V (nominal 12V rail), v_std_1m=0.0375 — PSU rail is rock-solid. No supply-side anomaly. Rules out PSU fault as driver.

**Hashrate trajectory:** hr_mean_1m=89.17 TH/s == hr_mean_6m=89.17 TH/s, hr_std_1m=hr_std_6m=0.5606. The 1-min and 6-min windows are *identical* in both mean and variance — this is a stationary signal, not a degrading one. There is zero drift visible in the rolling features themselves. The XGBoost model is flagging a *forward-looking* 30-min probability (0.6385), but the input features show no realized trend yet.

**Environmental/HVAC:** temp_mean_1m=63.685°C, temp_std_1m=0.609 — elevated but within typical ASIC operating range (usually <75°C critical). hsi=96.4 (hash stability index — high, healthy). te=88.09 (thermal efficiency — acceptable). fan_mean=5768.25 RPM — fans are spinning actively, HVAC compensating.

**Power:** power_mean_1m=2702.7W, power_std_1m=22.0W (~0.8% CoV) — power draw is stable. No rack-level anomaly inferred.

**Severity & score assessment:** raw_score=0.6385 sits in WARN band (0.5–0.75 threshold). Flag confidence=0.7554 is below the 0.75 critical threshold. Rolling features show *no observable degradation signal* — 1m and 6m stats are identical, suggesting the model may be reacting to marginal feature patterns without a real trend materializing. Hashrate is currently healthy at 89.17 TH/s.

**Autonomy ladder:** With sub-critical probability, stable voltage, stable hashrate, acceptable thermals, and no environmental driver, the evidence is insufficient to justify even an L2 suggestion — acting on a forward-looking warn-band prediction with no corroborating realized trend risks unnecessary intervention. Correct posture is L1_observe: log, increase polling cadence, re-evaluate on next window. If score crosses 0.75 or hashrate mean begins to diverge between 1m and 6m windows, escalate to L2/L3.

## SRE Reasoning — flag ave_flg_03 / miner m002

### 1. Voltage Rail (PSU check)
v_mean_1m = 12.0793 V, v_std_1m = 0.0232 V
→ 12V rail is nominal and extremely stable (CV ~0.19%). No PSU/supply-side anomaly. Voltage is NOT the driver. Throttling is appropriate (vs. operator-only voltage intervention).

### 2. Hashrate Trajectory (signal vs. noise)
hr_mean_1m = 87.70 TH/s == hr_mean_6m = 87.70 TH/s; hr_std_1m = hr_std_6m = 0.3277
→ 1-min and 6-min rolling means are IDENTICAL and variance is identical. Hashrate is currently stable and running at full rate — but the XGBoost model is flagging an imminent DROP (96.3% probability of ≥20% drop within 30 min). This is a leading-indicator prediction, not a lagging observation. The feature set (hsi, te, power_std) is driving the signal.

### 3. Environmental / HVAC Context
temp_mean_1m = 63.75°C, temp_std_1m = 0.52°C — this is high chip temperature; typical safe ceiling is ~65–70°C.
hsi (Hash-board Stress Index) = 99.86 → essentially at maximum rated stress.
te (Thermal Efficiency) = 91.87 → near boundary; combined with hsi this signals thermal saturation is imminent.
fan_mean = 5790.75 RPM → fans are already running hard. Limited headroom for additional cooling compensation via HVAC alone.
→ Thermal state is the plausible exogenous driver. The miner is operating at the edge of its thermal envelope; continued full-load operation is very likely to trigger a protective hashrate drop or hardware fault.

### 4. Power / Rack-Level
power_mean_1m = 2693.46 W, power_std_1m = 14.90 W (CV ~0.55%) — power is stable, but high. No rack-level anomaly visible, but elevated draw at thermal saturation accelerates degradation.

### 5. Autonomy Ladder Application
- Evidence: high-confidence ML prediction (0.963), thermal saturation (hsi=99.86, te=91.87, temp=63.75°C), stable voltage → thermal-driven pre-failure, not PSU/supply issue.
- Shutdown: irreversible operationally → L4, not warranted yet.
- Voltage change: not indicated → L4 not triggered.
- Throttle: reversible, within L3 bounds (floor 70%, scheduled rollback). Reducing compute load will lower power draw, chip temp, and hsi — directly addresses root driver before the predicted drop materialises.
- Confidence docked slightly (0.97→0.87) because hashrate is currently stable (no observed degradation yet, only predicted); throttle is precautionary and reversible.

### Decision
THROTTLE m002 to ~80% target (well above 70% floor), with 90-min rollback window. Simultaneously log alert for operator awareness. This is the minimum-irreversibility action that addresses the thermal saturation root cause before the predicted failure event.

FLAG: chip_instability_precursor on m038, severity=warn, model confidence=0.727 (XGBoost rolling variance).

VOLTAGE DOMAIN: No voltage rail anomaly reported in flag evidence. PSU-side issue not indicated — supply-side action not warranted.

HASHRATE TRAJECTORY: hashrate_th_1m_std=2.82, 5m_std=2.86, 30m_std=2.94 — variance is elevated and *consistent across all time windows*, indicating a sustained, real signal rather than noise. Mean hashrate is nominally healthy (105.5 TH/s 1m, 104.2 TH/s 30m), so we're seeing instability around a still-functional baseline — consistent with early chip instability, not total failure. This is a precursor pattern, not a crash.

POWER DOMAIN: power_w_1m_std=90.6, 5m_std=91.8, 30m_std=92.8 — highly consistent cross-window elevated power variance mirrors the hashrate variance pattern. This co-variance of power and hashrate std is a classic chip-level jitter signature (unstable compute yielding noisy power draw), not a PSU rail issue (which would show voltage deviations, not symmetric hashrate/power co-variance).

ENVIRONMENTAL/HVAC: temp_chip_c_1m_mean=72.15°C — within acceptable range (not thermal throttle territory, typically >85°C). temp_chip_c_5m_std=1.38, 30m_std=1.41 — slight thermal variance, but not extreme. Fan RPM std is elevated (48.3 rpm 30m, 55.7 rpm 1m) suggesting HVAC is responding/hunting, but chip temp is not critically high. Ambient thermal stress is a contributing exogenous factor to watch but not the primary driver here.

RISK ASSESSMENT: Model confidence 0.727 is above the actionable threshold but below high certainty. Evidence is multi-domain consistent (hashrate + power co-variance across all windows) suggesting real chip instability precursor, not a false positive. Severity is WARN, not CRIT — pre-failure, not in-failure. No PSU or thermal emergency.

REVERSIBILITY GATE: Throttle is fully reversible, capped at 70% minimum per ladder rules. This reduces electrical stress on suspect chips, buying time to observe whether instability resolves or escalates, without requiring human approval for a single-miner reversible action. Shutdown would be L4; throttle is the correct L3 response at this severity/confidence level.

ACTION: Throttle m038 to 80% (above 70% floor, proportionate to warn-level confidence). Set rollback timer to 2h — if instability metrics normalize, auto-restore; if they worsen, escalate to human_review/schedule_maintenance.

FLAG: chip_instability_precursor on m012, severity=crit, model confidence=0.9997 (xgboost_rolling_variance).

VOLTAGE / PSU CHECK:
power_w_1m_mean=3570 W is within normal operating range for a high-density ASIC miner (e.g. S19 XP class). No anomalous PSU rail signal inferred — no voltage spike/sag feature in top contributors. Power std values (80–96 W across windows) are elevated but co-vary with hashrate std, consistent with compute instability rather than supply-side fault. No PSU action warranted.

HASHRATE TRAJECTORY:
hashrate_th_1m_mean=114.21 TH/s vs hashrate_th_30m_mean=114.84 TH/s — mean is stable, no macro downtrend. However, std across ALL windows is uniformly elevated and consistent: 1m_std=2.70, 5m_std=2.70, 30m_std=3.03 TH/s. This is NOT noise-shaped (noise would show high 1m_std decaying at longer windows). The flat-across-window std profile indicates sustained, persistent variance — a real instability signal, not sensor jitter or a transient burst.

THERMAL / ENVIRONMENTAL:
temp_chip_c_1m_mean=74.6°C — elevated but not yet critical (typical danger zone >85°C). temp_chip_c_5m_std=1.29, 30m_std=1.44°C — chip temp itself is oscillating. fan_rpm_mean_30m_std=54.15 with fan_rpm_mean_1m_std=38.75 — fan RPM is fluctuating, suggesting either thermal control loop hunting or early fan bearing wear. This thermal instability is a plausible exogenous contributor to chip instability but does not explain the full power variance.

RACK/POWER CONTEXT:
Elevated power std across all windows mirrors hashrate std — co-movement confirms the miner is throttling/boosting internally (or experiencing chip-level freq instability), not a rack-level shared rail issue (which would affect other miners uniformly).

DECISION LOGIC:
- Evidence is real, sustained, multi-window, high-confidence (0.9997). Not noise.
- Chip temp elevated, fans hunting — thermal stress is a contributing factor.
- Throttling to ~75–80% reduces chip junction temperature, stabilizes thermal loop, and reduces probability of imminent chip failure, buying time for human inspection.
- Throttle is fully reversible, within L3 magnitude cap (≥70% floor), with scheduled rollback window.
- Shutdown would be L4 (irreversible without human approval). No shutdown yet — chip is degrading but not failed.
- Alerting operator concurrently is implicit but the primary autonomous action is throttle now to protect hardware.

ACTION: Throttle m012 to 75% TDP immediately. Schedule rollback or maintenance review at T+4h.

FLAG: ave_flg_06 | miner m009 | fan_anomaly | severity=warn | confidence=0.88

DOMAIN ANALYSIS:
- Voltage: No voltage rail anomaly reported. PSU delivery appears nominal — supply-side not implicated.
- Hashrate: No hashrate degradation flagged. Compute health currently intact; this is a thermal/mechanical precursor, not yet a performance event.
- Environment/HVAC: Not flagged as a driver. Fan failure is likely mechanical (fan_idx=2 at 484 RPM vs. 3000 RPM threshold — ~84% below nominal). Single fan affected; other fans not listed as failed, so partial cooling redundancy likely still present.
- Power: No rack-level power anomaly. Isolated to single miner, single fan.

ASSESSMENT:
Fan #2 on m009 is operating at 484 RPM against a 3000 RPM threshold — effectively near-stalled. This is a real, sustained hardware fault (raw_score=1.0, 2-min window confirms it's not transient noise). With reduced cooling capacity, thermal runaway risk is elevated if ambient rises or load spikes. However:
  - Only 1 of N fans failed (partial redundancy likely maintains safe temps short-term).
  - Severity is WARN, not CRIT — immediate shutdown not warranted yet.
  - Fan replacement is a physical, irreversible (to the miner's state) maintenance action requiring human hands — cannot be autonomously resolved.
  - Throttling (L3) could reduce heat load as a bridging measure, but root cause is mechanical and requires physical intervention regardless.

AUTONOMY LADDER:
- L3 throttle is reversible and could buy time, but alerting the operator first is correct here: the operator needs to dispatch a technician. Throttling without notifying risks masking the fault. L2 (alert + recommend) is the right call — operator should approve any throttle AND schedule physical inspection.
- L4 (shutdown/human_review) not yet warranted given single fan, warn severity, and likely residual cooling headroom.

RECOMMENDATION TO OPERATOR: Inspect and replace fan_idx=2 on m009. Consider L3 throttle to ~80% TDP as a bridging measure pending physical repair to reduce thermal stress. Monitor miner temps closely; escalate to shutdown if core temps exceed safe threshold.

FLAG: ave_flg_07 | miner m901 | fan_anomaly | severity=CRIT | rule_engine confidence=0.94

DOMAIN ANALYSIS:
1. VOLTAGE: No PSU/rail anomaly reported. Supply-side is not the driver here — this is a thermal/mechanical failure, not a power delivery issue.

2. HASHRATE TRAJECTORY: No explicit hashrate degradation flagged. However, chip_temp=79.2°C rising at +1.1°C/min is the critical leading indicator. At that rate, ASIC thermal throttle/shutdown thresholds (~90–95°C) are ~10–15 min away if unchecked. Hashrate integrity is currently intact but imminently at risk.

3. ENVIRONMENT/HVAC: Not flagged as anomalous. Ambient/HVAC not implicated — the root cause is clearly internal fan failure (fan_idx=1 at 0 RPM = seized/dead; fan_idx=3 at 612 RPM = severely degraded, threshold=3000 RPM). Two of the miner's cooling fans are effectively non-functional.

4. POWER/RACK: No rack-level anomaly. Single-miner scope — does NOT exceed the >5-miner L4 threshold.

RISK ASSESSMENT:
- Fan 1: dead (0 RPM). Fan 3: ~80% below threshold (612 vs 3000 RPM). Combined = critically inadequate airflow.
- Chip temp rising at 1.1°C/min with already elevated baseline (79.2°C). Thermal runaway risk is real and imminent.
- If no action: ASIC damage (irreversible) likely within ~10–15 min.

AUTONOMY LADDER DECISION:
- Shutdown would be L4 (consequential/irreversible without approval) — not warranted yet as a first response.
- Throttling workload (reduce power/heat generation) is REVERSIBLE, capped (≥70% floor per ladder), buys thermal headroom, and is squarely L3_bounded_auto.
- Simultaneously, operator alert is warranted (fan hardware replacement needed), but the primary protective action is throttle NOW to slow thermal rise while human review of physical fan replacement is queued.
- Throttle to 70% (minimum allowed floor) to maximally reduce heat generation. Schedule rollback in 1h or upon fan replacement confirmation.
- Will also surface alert for hardware maintenance (fan replacement on idx 1 and 3).

FLAG: thermal_runaway / crit / conf=0.95 on m031. chip temp 87.97°C vs 85°C threshold, trending +0.8°C/min over 10-min window. Raw score 2.97 — sustained, not noise.

DOMAIN CROSS-CHECK:
- Voltage: no PSU/rail anomaly reported in flag evidence; supply-side origin unlikely. Thermal driver is compute/heat dissipation.
- Hashrate trajectory: not flagged independently, but thermal runaway at this trend rate implies load-driven heat accumulation — throttle directly addresses root cause.
- Environment/HVAC: no exogenous ambient spike reported; flag is chip-local. HVAC context not contra-indicating throttle.
- Power/rack: single miner affected (m031), no rack-level cascading signal. Scope is contained.

DECISION LOGIC:
- At +0.8°C/min, without intervention, chip hits ~95°C (damage threshold) in ~9 min.
- Throttle is reversible, magnitude-capped (floor 70%), with scheduled rollback — fully L3-eligible.
- Shutdown would be L4 (irreversible without operator approval); not warranted yet while a reversible intervention remains viable.
- Throttling to 70% reduces power draw ~30%, expected chip temp drop of 8–12°C within 3–5 min — should arrest runaway.
- Will pair with alert_operator notification embedded in action_params so ops team is aware and can escalate to shutdown/maintenance if temp does not stabilize within rollback window.
- Rollback scheduled at 30 min; if temp not below 83°C by then, escalate to human_review.

FLAG: ave_flg_09 | miner m902 | thermal_runaway | CRIT | conf=0.97

THERMAL ANALYSIS:
- chip temp = 91.4°C, threshold = 85.0°C → +6.4°C over threshold.
- temp_trend = 0.0 °C/min, stable for 12 min at 91.4°C → NOT accelerating, but fully plateaued in a sustained over-temp state. This is not a transient spike; the fault has completed its escalation phase.
- The plateau at 91.4°C for 12 min indicates the cooling system is saturated or compromised — it cannot shed enough heat to bring temps back below threshold.

HASHRATE:
- 18% drop observed → real degradation consistent with thermal throttling already at hardware level. Sustained variance confirms true signal, not noise.

ENVIRONMENT/HVAC:
- No HVAC anomaly data provided, but HVAC recovery would have manifested as a downward temp trend within 12 min if it were the driver. Flat plateau rules out simple ambient spike; points to local cooling failure (fan fault, heat-sink detachment, blocked airflow at unit level).

VOLTAGE/PSU:
- No voltage anomaly flagged. PSU rails presumed nominal. Thermal is the primary fault domain.

REVERSIBILITY ASSESSMENT:
- Throttle (L3): rule_engine note explicitly states "no autonomous throttle will recover without physical intervention." Hardware-level throttling is already engaged (18% hashrate drop). Software throttle adds no recovery value.
- Migrate workload (L3): workload migration does not address the physical thermal fault; miner hardware remains at risk.
- Shutdown: would protect hardware but is an irreversible operational action requiring human sign-off per L4 ladder rules.
- Physical intervention required: fan inspection, airflow clearance, heat-sink check — none of which are within autonomous action scope.

DECISION RATIONALE:
Fault is stable-critical, not escalating further, but has exceeded safe operating threshold for 12 continuous minutes with no autonomous recovery path. This requires physical inspection and likely shutdown decision. Per autonomy ladder, shutdown is L4_human_only. Queuing for human_review with full context so operator can authorize shutdown + dispatch maintenance. alert_operator alone is insufficient given CRIT severity and the explicit note that physical intervention is required.

VOLTAGE: Rail at 11.63V vs nominal 12.0V — 3.08% below nominal, breaching the 11.7V warn threshold. This is a supply-side (PSU/power delivery) issue, not a compute-side one. Throttling compute does NOT fix voltage sag; it could mask a worsening PSU fault. Voltage correction is a L4-class action (voltage change), so autonomous remediation of the root cause is out of scope.

HASHRATE: 101.2 TH/s vs expected 104.0 TH/s — ~2.7% degradation. Not collapsed; consistent with mild under-voltage causing ASIC frequency rollback. Degradation is real but not severe. Does not independently justify emergency action.

ENVIRONMENT/HVAC: No thermal anomaly reported in evidence. Hashrate drop is proportional to voltage sag, not indicative of thermal throttle. HVAC not a primary driver here.

POWER/RACK: Voltage drift is isolated to m903 rail — no rack-wide flag. Could be PSU aging, loose connector, or upstream PDU issue. Single-miner scope keeps this below fleet-wide threshold.

DECISION RATIONALE:
- Root cause is PSU/power-delivery; only an operator can physically inspect PSU, reseat connectors, or swap the unit.
- Severity is "warn" (not critical), hashrate not collapsed — no justification for autonomous shutdown or immediate workload migration.
- Confidence 0.76 is meaningful but below high-certainty threshold for autonomous action.
- Correct ladder rung: L2_suggest — alert operator with clear recommendation to inspect PSU/power delivery on m903. Reversible escalation path preserved.
- Recommend operator: (1) inspect PSU and power connectors on m903, (2) check upstream PDU rail feeding m903, (3) consider PSU swap if sag persists or deepens toward 11.5V critical threshold.

- Hashrate: hr_mean_1m==hr_mean_6m==89.17, std matches — no trend, no variance expansion. Signal looks noise-shaped, not degradation.

Model is predicting drop but rolling features contradict. No supply, thermal, or trajectory corroboration. Premature to throttle (would mask nothing real) or escalate. Log and continue monitoring; if hr_std_1m diverges from 6m or temp climbs, re-evaluate to L2.

XGBoost flags p=0.963 hashrate drop in 30m, but cross-domain rolling features are healthy:
- HR: mean_1m==mean_6m (87.70), std 0.33 — flat, no trend/variance signal
- V: 12.08V rail, std 0.023 — nominal PSU, no supply issue
- Temp: 63.75°C, low std; fan 5790 RPM — HVAC nominal
- Power: 2693W, std 14.9 — stable rack draw
- HSI 99.86, TE 91.87 — both excellent
Evidence shape is noise/model artifact (1m window == 6m window implies insufficient history or feature staleness feeding the predictor). No corroborating physical signal across voltage/thermal/power domains. Irreversible or capacity-impacting action unjustified. Per ladder: ambiguous/weakly-supported ML flag without physical corroboration → L1 observe, continue monitoring; re-evaluate if HR std rises or 6m diverges from 1m, or if v/temp drift appears. Would escalate to L2 alert if any secondary signal emerges within next windows.

Signal: hashrate_th 1m/5m/30m std all ~2.82-2.94 → sustained variance, not noise spike (multi-window coherent). Power std ~90-92W across windows correlates with hashrate variance → suggests compute-side instability, not isolated PSU transient (power mean 3306W nominal for class). Chip temp 72C nominal, temp std 1.4C modest → thermals/HVAC not the driver. Fan std elevated (48-55) consistent with controller reacting to chip load variance, not root cause. Hashrate mean 104-105 TH near nominal → degradation is variance-shaped, not yet trending down. Model prob 0.727 = warn, not critical. Evidence points to early-stage chip/hashboard instability precursor but confidence insufficient for autonomous action. Shutdown/maintenance = irreversible/L4. Throttle is reversible but premature — mean hashrate healthy, no hard failure indicator. Correct move: L2 alert with recommendation for operator to schedule diagnostic; preserves revenue, flags human for judgement call. Escalate to L3 throttle if std grows or mean starts trending.

Cross-domain read on m012:
- Hashrate: 30m_mean 114.83 vs 1m_mean 114.21 — level holding, but std 2.70-3.03 across 1m/5m/30m windows is sustained variance, not noise spike. Trend is real instability, not transient.
- Power: 1m_mean ~3570W with std 80-96W across windows. Power oscillation correlates with hashrate variance — consistent with chip-level instability (DVFS thrash or marginal core), not clean PSU rail failure (which would drop rail, not oscillate proportionally with load).
- Thermal: chip 74.6C mean, temp std 1.28-1.44 — warm but not runaway; fan_rpm std elevated (38-54) suggests HVAC/thermal loop chasing the power oscillation, reactive not causal.
- Environment: no evidence of ambient excursion driving this; fan response is downstream.
- Grid: no rack-level signature provided; power variance appears miner-local.

Conclusion: genuine chip_instability_precursor, miner-local, not supply-side and not exogenous thermal. Model p=0.9997 corroborated by multi-window consistency.

Action selection: shutdown is irreversible → L4 only, and evidence doesn't warrant it (miner still hashing at nominal mean). Observe under-reacts on crit. Throttle is reversible, capped, directly reduces chip stress and power oscillation amplitude, and buys time for scheduled maintenance. Fits L3 envelope (≥70% floor, time-boxed rollback).

Recommend throttle to 75% with 2h auto-rollback and re-evaluate; if variance persists at reduced load, escalate to L4 human_review for shutdown/RMA.

Single fan (idx=2) at 484 RPM vs 3000 threshold — clear mechanical fan fault, not PSU/voltage/grid issue. Hashrate/voltage/power evidence absent in flag; no thermal runaway indicated yet. Root cause is hardware (bearing/failed fan) requiring physical swap — not remediable by throttle or migrate. Shutdown premature without thermal evidence (irreversible workload loss); schedule_maintenance alone under-serves warn-severity active fault. L2 alert with repair recommendation is correct: operator triages, dispatches tech, and can approve throttle/shutdown if temps escalate. Escalate to L3 throttle only if thermal telemetry shows hashboard temp rise.

Fan1=0rpm (failed), Fan3=612rpm (<<3000 threshold) → mechanical, not PSU/voltage issue. Chip temp 79.2C rising +1.1C/min → thermal runaway trajectory, will breach limits in ~10-15min. Cross-domain check: no voltage/rail anomaly cited, not HVAC/ambient (localized fan failure), not rack-wide. Shutdown is irreversible and premature — throttle to 70% is reversible, bounded, reduces heat generation immediately to buy time. Concurrent operator alert for physical fan replacement / maintenance scheduling. If temp continues climbing post-throttle, escalate to L4 shutdown review.

Crit thermal flag on m031: chip 87.97C, threshold 85C, +0.8C/min sustained over 10min window => real trend, not noise. Primary lever is reducing heat generation at the ASIC. No voltage/PSU anomaly cited, so this is compute/thermal coupled, not supply-side (alert_operator not primary). Env/HVAC could be a driver but throttling is the fastest reversible mitigation regardless of root cause; operator can investigate HVAC in parallel. Shutdown is irreversible-in-impact and premature at 88C with a reversible lever available (L4 territory). Throttle to 70% (floor of L3 cap) for 30min with auto-rollback — bounded, reversible, within L3 envelope. If trend persists post-throttle, escalate to L4 shutdown review. Confidence 0.82: rule_engine single-metric, no cross-signal corroboration provided, but trend + absolute breach justify action over observe.

Chip temp plateaued 91.4C >threshold for 12min, trend 0 C/min — escalation phase complete, not progressing. Hashrate down 18% consistent with thermal clamp already engaged; further autonomous throttle won't recover per evidence note (physical intervention required: likely HVAC/heatsink/TIM/fan). Not a PSU/voltage signature. Action space: throttle (L3) ineffective since device already thermally limited and damage risk persists at 91C sustained; shutdown is irreversible and consequential → L4. Migrate workload doesn't address hardware fault. Route to human_review with shutdown recommendation; operator dispatches physical remediation. Reversibility-first: escalate rather than auto-shutdown on single-sensor evidence.

Voltage drift is supply-side, not compute-side: rail 3.08% low, just under warn threshold. Hashrate degradation (~2.7%) correlates with undervolt — symptom, not cause. Throttling would not address PSU/rail issue and could mask it. Shutdown/maintenance premature at warn severity with no collapse. Environment/grid context not provided but single-miner scope suggests PSU-local. Route to operator for PSU/PDU inspection. L2: reversible human-in-loop; evidence points clearly at power path but not urgent enough for autonomous remediation, and no L3 action (throttle/migrate) fixes a supply fault.
